content:
The word **entropy** entered science through **thermodynamics** in the 19th century (**Clausius**, **Boltzmann**), measuring disorder in physical systems. **Claude Shannon** deliberately reused the name in his 1948 landmark paper **"A Mathematical Theory of Communication"**, defining **H = −Σ p log p** for discrete information sources and proving it governs the fundamental limit of lossless compression.

Shannon showed that the expected code length of any uniquely decodable source code is at least H(X) bits per symbol, with equality approached by optimal codes matched to the source PMF. This linked probability, uncertainty, and communication in a single formula.

**Hartley** (1928) had earlier proposed a logarithmic measure of information for equally likely outcomes; Shannon generalized it to arbitrary PMFs and unified noiseless coding with **channel capacity** in the same framework.

In the decades that followed, **Kullback**, **Leibler**, and others built **cross-entropy** and **KL divergence** on Shannon entropy to compare distributions—quantities now central to statistical inference and ML loss functions.

**Quinlan's ID3** (1986) brought entropy into mainstream ML through **information gain** for decision trees. Today, entropy and its extensions underpin classification loss, language-model perplexity, variational bounds, and information-theoretic regularization in deep learning.

timeline.label: 1928 — Hartley
timeline.description: Logarithmic measure of information for equiprobable events foreshadows Shannon's formula.

timeline.label: 1948 — Shannon
timeline.description: Defines H(X) = −Σ p log p; proves source coding theorem linking entropy to optimal compression.

timeline.label: 1951 — Kullback & Leibler
timeline.description: KL divergence and cross-entropy extend entropy to compare two distributions.

timeline.label: 1986 — ID3 decision trees
timeline.description: Information gain from entropy reduction becomes a standard feature-split criterion in ML.

timeline.label: Modern deep learning
timeline.description: Cross-entropy loss, perplexity, and mutual-information estimates operationalize Shannon entropy at scale in Gen AI systems.
